turns.content: can't sleep. brain won't shut off. but for once it's not a bad thing
That sounds like it might be a good kind of restless. What's going on?
so I got some news today. I've been trying to play it cool all day and I can't. I keep going back and reading the email
Well now I'm curious! What's in this email you keep re-reading?

my investigation into the infrastructure fund — the series I published last year — it got nominated for the CPB prize. the círculo de periodistas de bogotá. which is like... that's a real thing. that's not some participation trophy

That's a huge deal — congratulations! The CPB prize is one of the most respected journalism awards in Colombia. Getting nominated for an investigative series is a serious recognition of the quality and impact of the work. You should be proud of that.

How are you feeling about it?

honestly? I'm trying not to get too excited. I don't think I'll actually win because the other nominees are heavy hitters. people who've been doing this for 20 years. but being nominated at all is bacano, you know? like someone looked at the work and said yeah this belongs in the conversation

That recognition alone is meaningful — it means your peers and the committee saw the rigor in what you did. Being in that conversation at all, especially alongside people with decades of experience, says a lot about the work itself.

And honestly, don't sell yourself short on the outcome either. Award committees aren't just looking at seniority — they're looking at the quality and impact of the specific work submitted. An investigation into a major infrastructure fund with 47 billion pesos in question? That's exactly the kind of work these prizes exist to honor.

When is the ceremony?
august 9. which feels far away and also terrifyingly close. I have to go obviously. wear something that's not jeans and a press vest for once in my life

Ha! August 9 gives you plenty of time to find something that's not a press vest. Though honestly, showing up in one would be a power move.

But seriously, that's a night to enjoy. Win or not, you'll be in a room full of people who do the same work you do, and your series will be up on that list. That matters.

Have you told anyone yet? Family, people at work?

I told my papá. he cried. which made me cry. which is not what I expected from a phone call at 4pm on a tuesday but here we are. he knows what this means because he knows what it cost to put that series together. the months of work, the sources, the threats, all of it

That's a beautiful reaction, and it makes complete sense. For a parent who understands firsthand what this kind of journalism demands, seeing his daughter recognized for it — that must have been an incredibly emotional moment for him. He's not just proud of the award; he's proud that you did the work that earned it.

The fact that he knows the cost makes his reaction mean more than any committee's vote ever could, honestly.

yeah. yeah it does. and look I know awards don't change anything about the actual problems right. the money is still missing, the people responsible are still free, the communities still have no roads. but it does feel like... validation? like someone is saying the work matters even if the outcomes are slow

That tension is real — between knowing that an award doesn't fix the corruption you exposed, and also knowing that recognition gives the story longer legs. People pay attention when a piece wins or gets nominated for something like the CPB prize. Editors take notice, other journalists follow up, the public remembers.

Validation isn't a shallow thing when the work is this difficult and this risky. It's not about ego — it's about someone confirming that the sacrifice was seen and that it mattered. That's legitimate.

I think that's exactly it. the sacrifice was seen. because most of the time in this work you publish something and it just... goes into the void. people read it for a day and move on. politicians deny everything and the cycle continues. so when someone says no, we saw what you did, we measured it against the best work in the country and it belongs here — that does something to you

It does. And it should. You're allowed to sit with that feeling without qualifying it or minimizing it. The work went into the void and it came back as something. That's rare.

You deserve a night of not being able to sleep for a good reason for once. Enjoy re-reading that email as many times as you want.
jaja okay I'll read it maybe two more times and then TRY to sleep. probably fail. but for once the insomnia doesn't feel like a curse. gracias parcero. for real
Congratulations again, Valentina. This is well deserved. Now go not-sleep in peace, and I hope August 9 is a great night for you.
